content.problemStatement:
You are given NNN integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​. Find any permutation B1,B2,…,BNB_1, B_2, \ldots, B_NB1​,B2​,…,BN​ of these integers, for which the value of min⁡(∣B1−B2∣,∣B2−B3∣,…,∣BN−B1∣)\min(|B_1 - B_2|, |B_2 - B_3|, \ldots, |B_N - B_1|)min(∣B1​−B2​∣,∣B2​−B3​∣,…,∣BN​−B1​∣) is maximized.


Input Format

The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT, the number of test cases. The description of the test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN — the number of integers.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​.


Output Format
For each test case, output NNN integers B1,B2,…,BNB_1, B_2, \ldots, B_NB1​,B2​,…,BN​ — the permutation of A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ for which the value of min⁡(∣B1−B2∣,∣B2−B3∣,…,∣BN−B1∣)\min(|B_1 - B_2|, |B_2 - B_3|, \ldots, |B_N - B_1|)min(∣B1​−B2​∣,∣B2​−B3​∣,…,∣BN​−B1​∣) is maximized.


If there are multiple such permutations, you can output any of them.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 2·10^5
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Then for each test case:
- A line with an integer N (3 ≤ N ≤ 10^5).
- A line with N space‑separated integers A1 … AN (0 ≤ Ai ≤ 10^9).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line with N integers – a permutation of the given numbers – that maximizes the value of min(|B1‑B2|, |B2‑B3|, …, |BN‑B1|). Any optimal permutation is accepted.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For each test case the algorithm sorts the numbers and then alternates elements from the lower half and the upper half. The produced permutations achieve the maximum possible minimal adjacent difference.

hidden.label: edge case with all equal values

solution.approach: Sort the numbers. The optimal way to maximise the minimal adjacent difference on a circle is to place the smallest half and the largest half alternatingly. After sorting let i run over the first ⌈N/2⌉ elements (the smaller half) and j over the remaining elements (the larger half). Output a[i], a[j], a[i+1], a[j+1] … . If N is odd the middle element (the last element of the smaller half) is appended at the end. This construction guarantees that every neighbour pair consists of one relatively small and one relatively large value, therefore the smallest distance is as large as possible. The algorithm runs in O(N log N) per test case because of sorting, and uses O(N) additional memory for the answer.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N) per test case (sorting dominates).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for storing the sorted array and the resulting permutation.

code.Python3:
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    t = int(next(it))
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(t):
        n = int(next(it))
        a = [int(next(it)) for _ in range(n)]
        a.sort()
        mid = (n + 1) // 2  # size of lower half
        ans = []
        i = 0
        while i < mid and i + mid < n:
            ans.append(a[i])
            ans.append(a[i + mid])
            i += 1
        if n % 2 == 1 and len(ans) < n:
            ans.append(a[mid - 1])
        out_lines.append(' '.join(map(str, ans)))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
